name: Comarca de Omaña – circular from Montrondo through the Omaña Valley and the Valle Chico
name_original: Comarca de Omaña – circular desde Montrondo por el Valle de Omaña y el Valle Chico
region_intro: Touring cycling around Murias De Paredes offers routes through the western Leonese Mountains, characterized by a rugged relief with peaks exceeding 2,000 meters. The region encompasses three distinct valleys: Omaña, Valle Gordo, and El Valle Chico, providing varied terrain. Cyclists can explore well-preserved birch forests, notably along the Omaña River, and routes that traverse extensive meadows and follow smaller rivers. This area is part of the Omaña and Luna Valleys Biosphere Reserve, highlighting its natural diversity. Best touring cycling routes around Murias De Paredes The most popular touring cycling route is Comarca de Omaña – circular from Montrondo through the Omaña Valley and the Valle Chico, a 17.7 miles (28.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 45 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers scenic views through the region's valleys. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Las Rozas Reservoir – Matalavilla Lake loop from Villablino, a difficult 32.1 miles (51.7 km) path. This route features significant elevation changes and passes by a reservoir and lake. Local touring cyclists also love the Caboalles de Abajo loop from Café La Terraza, a 16.3 miles (26.2 km) trail leading through local villages and landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 21 minutes. Touring cycling around Murias De Paredes is defined by mountainous terrain, birch forests, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Murias De Paredes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 170 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Murias De Paredes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Murias De Paredes?
A: There are over 25 touring cycling routes around Murias De Paredes, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. You'll find routes traversing mountainous terrain, serene birch forests, and picturesque river valleys.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Murias De Paredes?
A: The terrain in Murias De Paredes is quite varied, reflecting its location in the western Leonese Mountains. You can expect rugged relief with significant elevation changes, but also routes that traverse valleys and extensive meadows. Surfaces can include gravel, ground, road, and track, offering diverse cycling experiences.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes for beginners in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, there is at least one easy touring cycling route suitable for beginners. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39558739" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caboalles de Abajo loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter route with less elevation gain, perfect for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Murias De Paredes?
A: Given the mountainous terrain, the best time for touring cycling in Murias De Paredes is generally from late spring through early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is typically milder and more stable, making for more enjoyable rides through the valleys and forests. Winters can be cold with snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in Murias De Paredes are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037750977/comarca-de-omana-circular-from-montrondo-through-the-omana-valley-and-the-valle-chico" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Comarca de Omaña – circular from Montrondo through the Omaña Valley and the Valle Chico&lt;/a&gt;, which offers scenic views through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling in Murias De Paredes?
A: Murias De Paredes is rich in natural beauty. You can cycle through some of the best-preserved birch forests in León, particularly near Fasgar, Montrondo, and along the Omaña River. The region is also home to the Vivero waterfall, formed by the Omaña River, which makes for a refreshing stop. The area is part of the Omaña and Luna Valleys Biosphere Reserve, highlighting its diverse ecosystems.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. Beyond natural landscapes, you can encounter historical sites. The village of Murias de Paredes itself features traditional stone houses and the historic Church of San Pedro. You might also pass by the medieval bridge of "La Lechería" or explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2611232" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palace of Quiñones&lt;/a&gt;, which is part of the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31874811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Emiliano – Palace of Quiñones loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about cycling in Murias De Paredes?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the stunning natural landscapes, the tranquility of the region, and the varied terrain that caters to different skill levels, from challenging mountain ascents to gentle valley rides.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by lakes or reservoirs?
A: Yes, some routes incorporate beautiful water features. For instance, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31450943" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Rozas Reservoir – Matalavilla Lake loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt; takes you past both a reservoir and a lake, offering picturesque views and a chance to enjoy the serene waterscapes.

Q: Are there any routes that are particularly challenging for experienced touring cyclists?
A: For experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, Murias De Paredes offers several difficult routes with significant elevation changes. The region's rugged relief includes peaks exceeding 2,000 meters, providing demanding ascents and descents. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31874811" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Emiliano – Palace of Quiñones loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt; is one such difficult route, covering over 60 km with substantial climbing.

Q: Can I find routes that are suitable for families with children?
A: While many routes in Murias De Paredes involve varied terrain and elevation, the easy routes are generally more suitable for families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39558739" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caboalles de Abajo loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a shorter, easier option that families might enjoy, allowing for a pleasant ride through local landscapes without excessive difficulty.

Q: Where can I find information about local services like bike rental or accommodation?
A: While specific bike rental services are not detailed in this guide, Murias De Paredes is appreciated for its tranquility and local gastronomy. For accommodation and other services, it's advisable to check local tourism resources or search for options in the main village of Murias de Paredes or nearby towns like Villablino, which serve as starting points for some routes.
tips: In this town you can visit a traditional mill and dairy. You can also make a small detour and look at its impressive Acebos holly tree.

Flanked by some of the best-preserved birch forests in the entire region, this town is the head of a municipality that includes fourteen towns spread over three valleys, Omaña and the Chico and Gordo valleys. It is one of those areas that has suffered hard from the scourge of depopulation, but once you are here you still want to change the statistics and stay to live.
Small town nestled between the mountains of the Omaña region, one of the most remote corners of the region. Some of the nearby peaks exceed two thousand meters, such as Catoute or Nevadín.
